Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

IMPLICIT BIAS OF PROJECTED SUBGRADIENT METHOD GIVES PROVABLE ROBUST RECOVERY OF SUBSPACES OF UNKNOWN CODIMENSION
**Paris Giampouras, Benjamin D. Haeffele and René Vidal** Mathematical Institute for Data Science Johns Hopkins University Baltimore, MD, USA {parisg,bhaeffele,rvidal}@jhu.edu

## ABSTRACT

Robust subspace recovery (RSR) is the problem of learning a subspace from sample data points corrupted by outliers. Dual Principal Component Pursuit (DPCP) is a robust subspace recovery method that aims to find a basis for the orthogonal complement of the subspace by minimizing the sum of the distances of the points to the subspaces subject to orthogonality constraints on the basis. Prior work has shown that DPCP can provably recover the correct subspace in the presence of outliers as long as the true dimension of the subspace is known. In this paper, we show that if the orthogonality constraints –adopted in previous DPCP formulations– are relaxed and random initialization is used instead of spectral one, DPCP can provably recover a subspace of _unknown dimension_ . Specifically, we propose a very simple algorithm based on running multiple instances of a projected sub-gradient descent method (PSGM), with each problem instance seeking to find one vector in the null space of the subspace. We theoretically prove that under mild conditions this approach succeeds with high probability. In particular, we show that 1) all of the problem instances will converge to a vector in the nullspace of the subspace and 2) the ensemble of problem instance solutions will be sufficiently diverse to fully span the nullspace of the subspace thus also revealing its true unknown codimension. We provide empirical results that corroborate our theoretical results and showcase the remarkable implicit rank regularization behavior of the PSGM algorithm that allows us to perform RSR without knowing the subspace dimension.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Robust subspace recovery (RSR) refers to the problem of identifying an underlying linear subspace (with dimension less than the ambient data dimension) from sample data points that are potentially corrupted with outliers (i.e., points that do not lie in the linear subspace). Many methods for RSR have been proposed in the literature over the past several years (Xu et al., 2012; You et al., 2017a; Lerman & Maunu, 2018). Formulations based on convex relaxations and decompositions of the data matrix into a low-rank matrix plus a matrix of sparse corruptions – either entrywise-sparse corruptions as in Candès et al. (2011) or columnwise-sparse corruptions as in Xu et al. (2012); McCoy & Tropp (2011) – can, in certain situations, be shown to provably recover the true subspace when the dimension is unknown. However, these theoretical guarantees often require the dimension of the subspace, _d_ , to be significantly less than the ambient dimension of the data, _D_ , and these methods are not suitable for the more challenging regime of subspaces of high relative dimension (i.e., when _D[d][≈]_[1][).]

**Dual Principal Component Pursuit.** Recently, progress has been made towards solving the RSR problem in the high relative dimension regime by a formulation termed Dual Principal Component Pursuit (DPCP). As implied by its name, DPCP follows a _dual_ perspective of RSR by aiming to recover a basis for the orthogonal complement of the inliers’ subspace. As shown in (Tsakiris & Vidal, 2018), DPCP is provably robust in recovering subspaces of high relative dimension. However, a key limitation of DPCP is that it requires _a priori_ knowledge of the true subspace dimension.

DPCP was first formulated by Tsakiris & Vidal (2018) for handling subspaces of codimension _c_ = _D − d_ equal to 1 (i.e., the subspace is a hyperplane with dimension _d_ = _D −_ 1). In this case, DPCP is formulated as the optimization problem

which is nonconvex due to the spherical constraint imposed on the normal vector _**b** ∈_ S _[D][−]_[1] of the _D −_ 1 dimensional hyperplane. Tsakiris & Vidal (2018) showed that the global minimizer of (1) is a normal vector of the underlying true hyperplane when both inliers and outliers are well-distributed _or_ the ratio between the number of inliers and number of outliers is sufficiently small. Following a probabilistic point of view, (Zhu et al., 2018) presented an improved theoretical analysis of DPCP giving further insights on the remarkable robustness of DPCP in recovering the true underlying subspaces even in datasets heavily corrupted by outliers. Moreover, the authors introduced a projected subgradient method which converges to a normal vector of the true subspace at a linear rate.

**Recursive DPCP for known** _c >_ 1. Zhu et al. (2018) also proposed an extension to DPCP to subspaces with codimension _c >_ 1 via a projected subgradient algorithm that tries to learn _c_ normal vectors to the subspace in a recursive manner. Specifically, after convergence to a normal vector, the projected subgradient algorithm is initialized with a vector _orthogonal_ to the previously estimated normal vector. However, for that approach to be successful, knowledge of the true subspace codimension _c_ becomes critical. Specifically, if an underestimate of the true codimension _c_ is assumed the recovered basis for the null space, _**B**_[ˆ] , will fail to span the whole null space, _S⊥_ . On the other hand, an overestimate of _c_ will lead to columns of _**B**_[ˆ] corresponding to vectors that lie in _S_ .

**Orthogonal DPCP for known** _c_ . Zhu et al. (2019) proposed an alternative to (1) which attempts to solve for _c_ normal vectors to the subspace at once by minimizing the sum of the distances from the points to the subspace:

The authors also proposed an optimization algorithm based on the projected Riemannian subgradient method (RSGM), which builds on similar ideas as the projected subgradient method of Zhu et al. (2018) and enjoys a linear converge rate when the step size is selected based on a geometrically diminishing rule. Ding et al. (2021) provided a geometric analysis of (2) which shows the merits of DPCP in handling a) datasets highly contaminated by outliers (in the order of _M_ = _O_ ( _N_[2] )) and b) subspaces of high relative dimension. However, a key shortcoming of this approach is that, because all minimizers of (2) are orthogonal matrices, a prerequisite for recovering the correct orthogonal complement of the inliers subspace is the _a priori_ knowledge of the true codimension _c_ (see Fig. 1).

**Contributions.** In this work, we address this key limitation by proposing a framework that allows us to perform robust subspace recovery in the high relative subspace dimension regime **without** requiring _a priori_ knowledge of the true subspace dimension. In particular, our proposed approach is based on the simple idea of solving multiple, parallel instances of the DPCP formulation for subspaces of codimension one,

where _c[′]_ is assumed to be an upper bound of _c_ , i.e., _c[′] ≥ c_ . Contrary to (2), the objective function in (3) decouples over the columns _**b** i_ of matrix _**B**_ = [ _**b**_ 1 _**b**_ 2 _· · ·_ _**b** c′_ ] and thus can be solved in a parallel manner by independently applying a projected subgradient algorithm (referred to as PSGM) from _c[′]_ different _random initializations_ . Moreover, we observe that with random initialization we can get vectors sufficiently spread on the sphere that lead PSGM (initialized with those vectors) to return normal vectors of _S_ . These are all _linearly independent_ when _c[′] ≤ c_ and thus can span _S⊥_ when _c[′]_ = _c_ . If _c[′] > c_ then PSGM will return _c[′] − c_ redundant vectors that will still lie in _S⊥_ yet they will be linearly dependent (see Figure 1). That being said, we show that this simple strategy permits us to robustly recover the true subspace even without knowledge of the true codimension _c_ .

As is detailed in Sections 3 and 4, this remarkable behavior of PSGM originates from the _implicit bias_ that is induced in the optimization problem due to a) the relaxation of _orthogonality constraints_ in (3) and b) the _random initialization_ scheme that is adopted. Our specific contributions are as follows:

Second, we analyze the discrete version which is more challenging, yet of more practical interest, showing that iterates of DPCP-PSGM converge to a _scaled_ and _perturbed_ version of the initial matrix _**B**_[0] . ## 2 RELATED WORK

**Subspace Recovery.** Learning underlying low-dimensional subspace representations of data has been a central topic of interest in machine learning research. Principal Component Analysis (PCA) has been the most celebrated method of this kind and is based on the minimization of the perpendicular distances of the data points from the estimates linear subspace, Jolliffe & Cadima (2016). Albeit, it is originally formulated as nonconvex optimization problem, PCA can be easily solved in closed form using a singular value decomposition (SVD) operation (see e.g. Vidal et al. (2016)). Despite its great success, PCA is prone to failure when handling datasets that contain _outliers_ i.e., data points whose deviation from the inliers’ subspace is “large” in the _ℓ_ 2 norm sense.

**Robust Subspace Recovery (RSR).** To remedy this weakness of PCA _robust subspace recovery (RSR)_ methods attempt to identify the outliers in the dataset an recover the true underlying lowdimensional subspace of the inliers Lerman & Maunu (2018); Maunu et al. (2019). A classical approach to this problem is RANSAC Fischler & Bolles (1981), which is given a time budget and randomly chooses per iteration _d_ points and then fits a _d_ -dimensional subspace to those points and checks how the proposed subspace fits the remaining data points. RANSAC then outputs the subspace that agrees with the largest number of points. However, RANSAC’s reliance on randomly sampling points to propose subspaces can be highly inefficient when the number of outliers is high (as well as the fact that RANSAC also needs knowledge of the true subspace dimension, _d_ ). The need to tackle inherent shortcomings of RANSAC pertaining to computational complexity issues inspired alternative convex formulations of RSR, Xu et al. (2012); You et al. (2017b); Rahmani & Atia (2017); Zhang & Lerman (2014). In Xu et al. (2012) the authors decompose the data matrix as a sum of a low-rank and a column-sparse component. However, theoretical guarantees obtained for convex

formulations only hold for subspaces of relatively low-dimensional subspaces i.e., for _d ≪ D_ where _d_ and _D_ denote the subspace and the ambient dimension, respectively. To the best of our knowledge, existing RSR algorithms rely heavily on one of two key assumptions. 1) The subspace is very low-dimensional relative to the ambient dimension ( _d ≪ D_ ) or 2) _The subspace dimension is a priori known_ . Undoubtedly, the second hypothesis is rather strong in real-world applications, and many applications also do not satisfy the first assumption. Moreover, heuristic strategies for selecting the dimension of the subspace are hard to be applied in the RSR setting since they incur computationally prohibitive procedures, Lerman & Maunu (2018).

**Relation to Orthogonal Dictionary Learning (ODL).** Note that objective functions in the form of (3) show up beyond RSR problems i.e., in orthogonal dictionary learning (ODL), sparse blind deconvolution, etc., Qu et al. (2020). Specifically, based on a similar formulation the authors in Bai et al. (2019) proved that _c[′]_ = _O_ ( _c_ log _c_ ) independent random initial vectors suffice in order to recover with high probability a dictionary of size _D × c_ with high accuracy. In this paper we aim to recover a basis of the orthogonal complement of a subspace of unknown dimension instead of accurately estimating a dictionary hence our goal differs from that in Bai et al. (2019).

**Implicit bias in Robust Recovery Problems.** The notions of implicit bias and implicit regularization have been used interchangeably in the nonconvex optimization literature for describing the tendency of optimization algorithms to converge to global minima of minimal complexity with favorable generalization properties in overparameterized models, Gunasekar et al. (2018). In the context of robust recovery, the authors in You et al. (2020) showed that Robust PCA can be suitably reparametrized in such a way to favor low-rank and sparse solutions without using any explicit regularization. In this work, we use the term implicit bias for describing the convergence of DPCPPSGM to low-rank solutions, which are not necessarily global minimizers, that span the orthogonal complement of the subspace when a) orthogonality constraints in DPCP formulation are relaxed b) DPCP is overparameterized i.e., _c[′] ≥ c_ and c) PSGM randomly initialized.

## 4 DUAL PRINCIPAL COMPONENT PURSUIT IN SUBSPACES OF UNKNOWN CODIMENSION

Current theoretical results provide guarantees for recovering the true inlier subspace, when the proposed algorithms know _a priori_ of the subspace codimension _c_ , which is a rather strong requirement and is far from being true in real word applications. Here we describe our proposed approach, which consists of removing the orthogonality constraint on _**B**_ , along with a theoretical analysis that gives guarantees of recovering the true underlying subspace even when the true codimension _c_ is unknown. First we analyze a continuous version of DPCP, which arises when the number of inliers and outliers are distributed according to continuous measures and their number tends to _∞_ . The continuous DPCP incurs an optimization problem with a benign landscape that allows us to better illustrate the favorable properties of DPCP-PSGM when it comes to the convergence of its iterates. Then we extend the results to the discrete case that deals with a finite number of inliers and outliers yielding a more challenging optimization landscape.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Progressive (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: This paper presents a novel and conceptually elegant approach to robust subspace recovery (RSR) that successfully removes the critical requirement of knowing the true subspace dimension — a major limitation in prior Dual Principal Component Pursuit (DPCP) methods. The key insight — that replacing strict orthogonality constraints with random initialization in a parallelized projected subgradient method (PSGM) leads to implicit rank regularization and provable recovery of the full orthogonal complement without knowing the codimension — is both theoretically well-motivated and empirically compelling. The paper is clearly written, well-structured, and builds a strong narrative by systematically reviewing existing DPCP formulations and pinpointing their dependence on *a priori* knowledge of subspace dimension. The theoretical claims, particularly the two-part guarantee that each PSGM instance converges to a nullspace vector and that the ensemble spans the full nullspace under mild conditions, are significant contributions. However, a major concern lies in the incompleteness of the theoretical analysis: while the paper claims high-probability recovery and diversity of solutions, it lacks precise quantification of the required number of restarts $ c' $, the probability of capturing the full nullspace as a function of sample size and geometry, and explicit dependence on inlier/outlier distributions. Furthermore, the truncation of the paper at a critical point (ending mid-sentence in the last paragraph) suggests this submission is incomplete, undermining confidence in the full reproducibility and robustness of the results. Despite this, the core idea — that the implicit bias of PSGM under random initialization can replace explicit constraints — is innovative and has broad implications for optimization-based robust learning. A complete version of the paper with full proofs, clearer dependence on problem parameters, and more extensive comparisons with non-DPCP methods for unknown-dimension RSR would strengthen an already impactful contribution.

comments.title: Potential sign or scaling error in the projection evolution equation (11)
comments.explanation: Equation (11) claims: $ P_{S^\perp}(\mathbf{b}^{(k+1)}_i) = (1 - \mu^{(k)} p c_D) P_{S^\perp}(\hat{\mathbf{b}}^{(k)}_i) $. This suggests that the projection onto $ S^\perp $ gets multiplicatively scaled by $ (1 - \mu^{(k)} p c_D) $ at each step. However, in projected subgradient methods for DPCP, the gradient involves $ \text{Sgn}(\mathbf{O}^\top \mathbf{b}) \mathbf{O} $ for outliers, whose projection onto $ S^\perp $ drives $ \mathbf{b} $ toward $ S^\perp $. The update is typically $ \mathbf{b}_{k+1} \leftarrow \mathbf{b}_k - \mu_k \cdot \nabla f(\mathbf{b}_k) $, followed by normalization. The component of $ \mathbf{b}_k $ in $ S^\perp $ should increase in magnitude if the gradient has a component in that direction. A decay factor like $ (1 - \mu^{(k)} p c_D) $ would imply contraction, which contradicts the intuition unless $ p c_D $ is negative (which it isn't). The derivation likely assumes an average-case or expected gradient behavior, but as written, it appears that the projection is shrinking, which would prevent convergence to $ S^\perp $. However, given the authors’ later claim that the limit is a projection of the initial vector onto $ S^\perp $, the equation may represent the evolution of the component orthogonal to $ S^\perp $ decreasing, but the notation $ P_{S^\perp}(\mathbf{b}) $ clearly refers to the projection onto $ S^\perp $. This may be a sign error: it should perhaps be the component in $ S $ that decays as $ (1 - \mu \dots) $, while $ P_{S^\perp} $ grows. Without seeing the derivation, this is concerning. The claim that projections are scaled versions of previous ones could still be valid under certain assumptions, but the specific factor needs justification.

comments.title: Incorrect Expression for Recovered Normal Vectors in Lemma 4
comments.explanation:
At first glance, the expression for the limit vectors in Lemma 4 appears to be a typographical or OCR-induced error. The notation '∥PPS⊥S⊥' is nonsensical—likely an OCR misread of a projection operator applied iteratively. Given the preceding discussion, especially Equation (11), which states that projections onto $S^\perp$ evolve as scaled versions: $\mathbf{P}_{S^\perp}(\hat{\mathbf{b}}_i[k+1]) = (1 - \mu[k] i p c_D) \mathbf{P}_{S^\perp}(\hat{\mathbf{b}}_i[k])$, and that the initialization plays a key role, one would expect the limiting vector to be a normalized projection of the initial vector onto $S^\perp$, specifically $ \hat{\mathbf{b}}_i^* \propto \mathbf{P}_{S^\perp}(\mathbf{b}_i[0]) $, normalized to unit norm.

The text also says 'Lemmas 3 and 4 pave the way for Theorem 5', which asserts that the span of the converged vectors equals $S^\perp$. That would only be true if the $\mathbf{P}_{S^\perp}(\mathbf{b}_i[0])$ are sufficiently independent—something plausible under random uniform initialization.

However, the written formula appears syntactically broken: 'PPS⊥S⊥' may be a garbled version of '$\mathbf{P}_{S^\perp}$' repeated or misaligned by OCR, and the denominator norm in a normalization might have been confused with an operator. A plausible intended expression is:
$$
\hat{\mathbf{b}}_i^* = \frac{\mathbf{P}_{S^\perp}(\mathbf{b}_i[0])}{\|\mathbf{P}_{S^\perp}(\mathbf{b}_i[0])\|_2}
$$
or possibly involving a product of scaling factors from the recursion in (11). Yet even that would be an approximation under constant scaling. The current version cannot be interpreted as correct even under the best plausible symbol recovery. The double $P$ and $S\perp$ suggest misalignment.

No such formula appears in the continuous measure literature of DPCP to our knowledge, and the current form is mathematically ill-formed. This undermines the theoretical contribution of Lemma 4, which is meant to formally link initialization to solution direction—a key claim about implicit bias.

comments.title: Incorrect Index in Step-Size Assumption in Algorithm and Convergence Analysis
comments.explanation:
In Algorithm 1 and in Equation (13), the step size is written as $\mu[k]_i$, suggesting it may depend on both iteration $k$ and vector index $i$. However, in the paper's earlier definition (Theorem 1, equation (8)), the step size is defined as $\mu[k] = \mu_0 \beta^{\lfloor k / K \rfloor}$, independent of $i$. This creates a conflict.

In the convergence analysis, in particular in Equation (14) and the recursive expansion of $\mathbf{P}_{S^\perp}(\mathbf{b}_i[K])$, the derivation appears to assume a single step size $\mu[k]$ per iteration, applied uniformly across columns. The theoretical guarantees in Theorem 1 (and its extension here) rely on a globally synchronized diminishing step size, not one varying across $i$.

Furthermore, in the expression for the perturbation dynamics (14), there is only $\mu[k]$ (not $\mu[k]_i$) used in the scaling. So, despite the algorithm stating $\mu[k]_i$, the analysis proceeds as if $\mu[k]$ is shared.

While it's possible the authors intend a shared step size and this is just inconsistent notation—e.g., $\mu[k]_i$ might mean $\mu[k]$ for all $i$—this introduces ambiguity. In optimization, index-dependent step sizes suggest per-coordinate or dual averaging schemes, which are not implied here. Worse, in Theorem 7, condition (18) involves $\mu_0$ and $K_0$, not indexed versions.

Thus, the use of $\mu[k]_i$ is misleading and not consistent with either the earlier step size rule or the analysis. It should be $\mu[k]$ for all $i$. This could confuse a reader trying to implement Algorithm 1.

comments.title: Misstatement of Convergence in Theorem 5
comments.quote: A PSGM algorithm initialized with _**B**_[0] _will almost surely converge to a matrix_ _**B**_[ˆ] _[∗] such that_ span( _**B**_[ˆ] _[∗]_ ) _≡S⊥._
comments.explanation:
Theorem 5 claims that the PSGM algorithm 'will almost surely converge to a matrix $\widehat{\mathbf{B}}^*$ such that $\text{span}(\widehat{\mathbf{B}}^*) \equiv S^\perp$'. However, this overreaches what is proven in Lemma 4 and the surrounding analysis.

From Lemma 4, under the continuous model, the limit vectors are aligned with $\mathbf{P}_{S^\perp}(\mathbf{b}_i[0])$, assuming the formula were correct. But even then, convergence of the span to $S^\perp$ requires that the projections $\mathbf{P}_{S^\perp}(\mathbf{b}_i[0])$ for $i=1,\dots,c'$ span $S^\perp$. If $c' > c$, this is possible, but if the initial vectors are such that their projections are linearly dependent, the span may not reach full rank $c$.

The claim is that random uniform initialization ensures this—plausible, but not directly justified here. Almost sure full-rank span of projections of $c'$ random vectors onto a $c$-dimensional subspace (here $S^\perp$) requires that $c' \geq c$ and the initial vectors are in general position, which occurs almost surely under uniform spherical distribution. While this is standard in random matrix theory, the paper does not state or cite this fact. Lemma 4 does not assert linear independence.

Moreover, Theorem 5 states 'span equals $S^\perp$' as a deterministic consequence, but this only holds almost surely due to the randomness in initialization. The phrase 'almost surely' should modify the entire conclusion (i.e., 'with probability 1, the span equals $S^\perp$'), but as written, it attaches only to convergence, not to the span condition.

Thus, the claim overstates the result by implying that convergence directly entails correct subspace recovery, without acknowledging the probabilistic dependency on initialization spread.

comments.title: Misleading Prose on Convergence of Discrete DPCP Iterates
comments.quote: Second, we analyze the discrete version which is more challenging, yet of more practical interest, showing that iterates of DPCP-PSGM converge to a scaled and perturbed version of the initial matrix **B**[0].
comments.explanation: The phrase 'scaled and perturbed version of the initial matrix **B**[0]' is inaccurate. The formal analysis shows convergence not to a direct transformation of **B**[0], but rather to $\mathbf{P}_{S^\perp}(\mathbf{B}_0)$ minus a perturbation matrix $\widehat{\mathbf{\Delta}}$ — a projection followed by asymptotic error accumulation, not a simple scaling. While 'scaled' might loosely refer to directional dependence via projection and 'perturbed' to $\widehat{\boldsymbol{\delta}}_i$, there is no uniform scaling of the full **B**[0], and the subspace projection fundamentally alters its structure. This risks misleading readers into thinking the output is a small deviation from the initialization, whereas in fact components in $S$ are entirely suppressed. Given that the statement appears in the contributions list and shapes reader expectations, it constitutes a moderate overstatement.

comments.title: Rank Recovery Claim Lacks Explicit Connection to Rank Estimation Method
comments.quote: The results corroborate the theory showing that the DPCP-PSGM with random initialization biases the solutions of **B**[ˆ] towards matrices with rank _c_.
comments.explanation: The claim that DPCP-PSGM recovers rank _c_ is central, yet the passage does not specify how the estimated codimension is extracted from **B**[ˆ]. Is it via rank thresholding (e.g., counting singular values above a tolerance)? Or by checking linear dependence of iterates? This is not described in the simulation section or Fig. 3's caption. While theoretical results suggest the limit should have rank _c_, the empirical validation assumes the reader knows how rank was numerically estimated. In practice, determining numerical rank is non-trivial, especially near the recovery threshold. Without mentioning the threshold or method (e.g., 'rank as number of singular values > 1e-6'), reproducibility is compromised. Given that estimating unknown _c_ is a core claim, and the method is omitted, this qualifies as underspecification.

(2018)) is missing from the passage. The placeholder '[1]' suggests a footnote or reference that is not included, and the update rule itself—expected to be of the form $\mathbf{b}_{k+1} \propto \mathbf{b}_k - \mu_k \cdot \text{Sgn}(\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{b}_k) \mathbf{X}$ projected onto the sphere—is not provided. While the earlier section mentions the update briefly, it's critical here in Section 4.1 where Lemma 3 and Lemma 4 refer to '(10)' and 'the steps described in (10)', but no such equation appears in the provided text. The omission is especially problematic because the convergence claims in Lemma 3 and 4 depend on the specific form of the update. However, since the paper references prior work and likely includes (10) elsewhere, and the general form is standard in DPCP literature, this may be a forward reference or extraction artifact rather than an intrinsic error. Still, in the provided passage, the lack of the update rule harms reproducibility.

comments.title: Potential sign or scaling error in the projection evolution equation (11)
comments.explanation: Equation (11) claims: $ P_{S^\perp}(\mathbf{b}^{(k+1)}_i) = (1 - \mu^{(k)} p c_D) P_{S^\perp}(\hat{\mathbf{b}}^{(k)}_i) $. This suggests that the projection onto $ S^\perp $ gets multiplicatively scaled by $ (1 - \mu^{(k)} p c_D) $ at each step. However, in projected subgradient methods for DPCP, the gradient involves $ \text{Sgn}(\mathbf{O}^\top \mathbf{b}) \mathbf{O} $ for outliers, whose projection onto $ S^\perp $ drives $ \mathbf{b} $ toward $ S^\perp $. The update is typically $ \mathbf{b}_{k+1} \leftarrow \mathbf{b}_k - \mu_k \cdot \nabla f(\mathbf{b}_k) $, followed by normalization. The component of $ \mathbf{b}_k $ in $ S^\perp $ should increase in magnitude if the gradient has a component in that direction. A decay factor like $ (1 - \mu^{(k)} p c_D) $ would imply contraction, which contradicts the intuition unless $ p c_D $ is negative (which it isn't). The derivation likely assumes an average-case or expected gradient behavior, but as written, it appears that the projection is shrinking, which would prevent convergence to $ S^\perp $. However, given the authors’ later claim that the limit is a projection of the initial vector onto $ S^\perp $, the equation may represent the evolution of the component orthogonal to $ S^\perp $ decreasing, but the notation $ P_{S^\perp}(\mathbf{b}) $ clearly refers to the projection onto $ S^\perp $. This may be a sign error: it should perhaps be the component in $ S $ that decays as $ (1 - \mu \dots) $, while $ P_{S^\perp} $ grows. Without seeing the derivation, this is concerning. The claim that projections are scaled versions of previous ones could still be valid under certain assumptions, but the specific factor needs justification.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for estimated subspace and convergence limit
comments.quote: will almost surely asymptotically converge to a matrix _**B**_[ˆ] _[∗] ∈_ R _[D][×][c][′] whose columns _**b**_[ˆ] _[∗] i[,][i]_[=][1] _[,]_[ 2] _[, . . . , c][′][will be normal vectors of] the inliers’ subspace
comments.explanation: The notation $\widehat{\mathbf{B}}^*$ introduced in Lemma 3 has not been previously defined in the provided text. While it's standard to use $*$ for limit points, the hat suggests an estimate, and this combined symbol appears without definition. More critically, earlier in Section 1, the recovered matrix is denoted $\widehat{\mathbf{B}}$, and in Theorem 5, $\widehat{\mathbf{B}}_0$ is used for initialization. The sudden introduction of $\widehat{\mathbf{B}}^*$ without explanation is inconsistent. However, the meaning is clear from context: it denotes the limit of the PSGM iterates. The use of both hat and star may be intentional to distinguish the limit from finite iterates. Given that this is common in optimization literature (e.g., $\mathbf{x}^*$ for minimizer), and the passage is otherwise consistent in subscripting with $i$, this is likely a stylistic choice rather than a real inconsistency. The potential confusion is minor and resolved by context.

comments.title: Unjustified step in reasoning from Lemma 4 to rank recovery
comments.quote: Lemma 4 shows that the initialization of PSGM plays a pivotal role since it determines the direction of the recovered normal vectors \{_**b**_[ˆ] _[∗] i[}][c] i_ =1 _[′]_[.][Lemmas 3 and 4 pave the way for Theorem 5.]
comments.explanation: Lemma 4 claims that the limit vectors are $ \widehat{\mathbf{b}}^*_i = \frac{ P_{S^\perp}(\widehat{\mathbf{b}}^{(0)}_i) }{ \| P_{S^\perp}(\widehat{\mathbf{b}}^{(0)}_i) \|_2 } $, i.e., normalized projections of the initial vectors. This is a strong claim: it says the final normal vectors depend only on the projection of the random initialization onto $ S^\perp $. If this holds, then since the initial $ \widehat{\mathbf{b}}^{(0)}_i $ are independent and uniform on the sphere, their projections $ P_{S^\perp}(\widehat{\mathbf{b}}^{(0)}_i) $ are independent Gaussian-like vectors in $ S^\perp $ (a $c$-dimensional space). Thus, with $c' \geq c$, the span of $ \{ P_{S^\perp}(\widehat{\mathbf{b}}^{(0)}_i) \}_{i=1}^{c'} $ will almost surely have dimension $ \min(c', c) = c $, since $c' \geq c$ and the vectors are randomly initialized. This would justify that $ \text{rank}(\widehat{\mathbf{B}}^*) = c $. However, the derivation of Lemma 4 is not provided in the passage—the equation (11) is given, but the transition from the iterative scaling to the closed-form expression for $ \widehat{\mathbf{b}}^*_i $ is missing. The reasoning involves solving a recurrence or taking the infinite product of the scaling factors, which depends critically on the step size rule (geometrically diminishing). The claim is plausible and aligns with the paper’s key argument about implicit bias, but the leap from (11) to the closed-form limit is non-trivial and lacks justification in the provided text.

comments.title: Misleading statement about Lemma 2 defining f_b with dependence on b_i
comments.quote: _where f_ _**b**_ : S _[D][−]_[1] _→_ R _, f_ _**b**_ ( **z** ) = _|_ **z** _[⊤]_ _**b** |, φi is the principal angle of_ _**b** i from the inliers subspace S and p is the probability of occurrence of an outlier._
comments.explanation: The description following Lemma 2 introduces $f_{\mathbf{b}}$, which depends on $\mathbf{b}$, but then refers to $\phi_i$, the principal angle of $\mathbf{b}_i$ from $S$, as if multiple $\mathbf{b}_i$ are involved. However, Lemma 2 is stated for the continuous version of (3), which involves a single vector $\mathbf{b}$ in the $c'=1$ case, or possibly multiple vectors in $\mathbf{B}$. The phrasing suggests $f_{\mathbf{b}}$ depends on $\phi_i$, but $f_{\mathbf{b}}(\mathbf{z}) = |\mathbf{z}^\top \mathbf{b}|$ does not explicitly involve $\phi_i$. The mention of $\phi_i$ here seems premature and confusing—it belongs in the analysis of the full $\mathbf{B}$ matrix with multiple columns. Most likely, this is a notational oversight in the prose: $\phi_i$ should be associated with $\mathbf{b}_i$ when discussing the multi-vector case in later sections, but it's oddly referenced here in a general lemma. Since the actual equation (9), not shown fully, may involve angular terms, this may be contextually justified. But as written, it introduces a symbol not used in the function definition, creating ambiguity.

comments.title: Incorrect Expression for Recovered Normal Vectors in Lemma 4
comments.explanation:
At first glance, the expression for the limit vectors in Lemma 4 appears to be a typographical or OCR-induced error. The notation '∥PPS⊥S⊥' is nonsensical—likely an OCR misread of a projection operator applied iteratively. Given the preceding discussion, especially Equation (11), which states that projections onto $S^\perp$ evolve as scaled versions: $\mathbf{P}_{S^\perp}(\hat{\mathbf{b}}_i[k+1]) = (1 - \mu[k] i p c_D) \mathbf{P}_{S^\perp}(\hat{\mathbf{b}}_i[k])$, and that the initialization plays a key role, one would expect the limiting vector to be a normalized projection of the initial vector onto $S^\perp$, specifically $ \hat{\mathbf{b}}_i^* \propto \mathbf{P}_{S^\perp}(\mathbf{b}_i[0]) $, normalized to unit norm.

The text also says 'Lemmas 3 and 4 pave the way for Theorem 5', which asserts that the span of the converged vectors equals $S^\perp$. That would only be true if the $\mathbf{P}_{S^\perp}(\mathbf{b}_i[0])$ are sufficiently independent—something plausible under random uniform initialization.

However, the written formula appears syntactically broken: 'PPS⊥S⊥' may be a garbled version of '$\mathbf{P}_{S^\perp}$' repeated or misaligned by OCR, and the denominator norm in a normalization might have been confused with an operator. A plausible intended expression is:
$$
\hat{\mathbf{b}}_i^* = \frac{\mathbf{P}_{S^\perp}(\mathbf{b}_i[0])}{\|\mathbf{P}_{S^\perp}(\mathbf{b}_i[0])\|_2}
$$
or possibly involving a product of scaling factors from the recursion in (11). Yet even that would be an approximation under constant scaling. The current version cannot be interpreted as correct even under the best plausible symbol recovery. The double $P$ and $S\perp$ suggest misalignment.

No such formula appears in the continuous measure literature of DPCP to our knowledge, and the current form is mathematically ill-formed. This undermines the theoretical contribution of Lemma 4, which is meant to formally link initialization to solution direction—a key claim about implicit bias.

comments.title: Incorrect Index in Step-Size Assumption in Algorithm and Convergence Analysis
comments.explanation:
In Algorithm 1 and in Equation (13), the step size is written as $\mu[k]_i$, suggesting it may depend on both iteration $k$ and vector index $i$. However, in the paper's earlier definition (Theorem 1, equation (8)), the step size is defined as $\mu[k] = \mu_0 \beta^{\lfloor k / K \rfloor}$, independent of $i$. This creates a conflict.

In the convergence analysis, in particular in Equation (14) and the recursive expansion of $\mathbf{P}_{S^\perp}(\mathbf{b}_i[K])$, the derivation appears to assume a single step size $\mu[k]$ per iteration, applied uniformly across columns. The theoretical guarantees in Theorem 1 (and its extension here) rely on a globally synchronized diminishing step size, not one varying across $i$.

Furthermore, in the expression for the perturbation dynamics (14), there is only $\mu[k]$ (not $\mu[k]_i$) used in the scaling. So, despite the algorithm stating $\mu[k]_i$, the analysis proceeds as if $\mu[k]$ is shared.

While it's possible the authors intend a shared step size and this is just inconsistent notation—e.g., $\mu[k]_i$ might mean $\mu[k]$ for all $i$—this introduces ambiguity. In optimization, index-dependent step sizes suggest per-coordinate or dual averaging schemes, which are not implied here. Worse, in Theorem 7, condition (18) involves $\mu_0$ and $K_0$, not indexed versions.

Thus, the use of $\mu[k]_i$ is misleading and not consistent with either the earlier step size rule or the analysis. It should be $\mu[k]$ for all $i$. This could confuse a reader trying to implement Algorithm 1.

comments.title: Misstatement of Convergence in Theorem 5
comments.quote: A PSGM algorithm initialized with _**B**_[0] _will almost surely converge to a matrix_ _**B**_[ˆ] _[∗] such that_ span( _**B**_[ˆ] _[∗]_ ) _≡S⊥._
comments.explanation:
Theorem 5 claims that the PSGM algorithm 'will almost surely converge to a matrix $\widehat{\mathbf{B}}^*$ such that $\text{span}(\widehat{\mathbf{B}}^*) \equiv S^\perp$'. However, this overreaches what is proven in Lemma 4 and the surrounding analysis.

From Lemma 4, under the continuous model, the limit vectors are aligned with $\mathbf{P}_{S^\perp}(\mathbf{b}_i[0])$, assuming the formula were correct. But even then, convergence of the span to $S^\perp$ requires that the projections $\mathbf{P}_{S^\perp}(\mathbf{b}_i[0])$ for $i=1,\dots,c'$ span $S^\perp$. If $c' > c$, this is possible, but if the initial vectors are such that their projections are linearly dependent, the span may not reach full rank $c$.

The claim is that random uniform initialization ensures this—plausible, but not directly justified here. Almost sure full-rank span of projections of $c'$ random vectors onto a $c$-dimensional subspace (here $S^\perp$) requires that $c' \geq c$ and the initial vectors are in general position, which occurs almost surely under uniform spherical distribution. While this is standard in random matrix theory, the paper does not state or cite this fact. Lemma 4 does not assert linear independence.

Moreover, Theorem 5 states 'span equals $S^\perp$' as a deterministic consequence, but this only holds almost surely due to the randomness in initialization. The phrase 'almost surely' should modify the entire conclusion (i.e., 'with probability 1, the span equals $S^\perp$'), but as written, it attaches only to convergence, not to the span condition.

Thus, the claim overstates the result by implying that convergence directly entails correct subspace recovery, without acknowledging the probabilistic dependency on initialization spread.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Definition of $\eta_{\mathbf{O}}$ and $\eta_{\mathbf{X}}$ in Extracted Text
comments.explanation:
The definition of $\eta_{\mathbf{O}}$ is written as $\frac{1}{M} \max_{\mathbf{b}} \|(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{b}\mathbf{b}^\top)\mathbf{O} \mathrm{Sgn}(\mathbf{O}^\top \mathbf{b})\|_2$, which is inconsistent with standard DPCP literature. From Tsakiris & Vidal (2018), the correct quantity should measure alignment of the subgradient, and typically involves the component of the subgradient orthogonal to $\mathbf{b}$, but normalized or structured differently.

However, upon cross-checking with the paper summary provided—which lists:
$\eta_{\mathbf{O}} = \frac{1}{M} \max_{\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{S}^{D-1}} \|(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{b}\mathbf{b}^\top)\mathbf{O} \mathrm{Sgn}(\mathbf{O}^\top \mathbf{b})\|_2$, — this appears to be the intended definition. The same is given for $\eta_{\mathbf{X}}$ with $\mathbf{P}_S - \mathbf{b}\mathbf{b}^\top$. The summary confirms this is not an error.

Still, the expression $\mathbf{P}_S - \mathbf{b}\mathbf{b}^\top$ in $\eta_{\mathbf{X}}$ is unusual: $\mathbf{P}_S$ and $\mathbf{b}\mathbf{b}^\top$ may not commute, and the operation $\mathbf{P}_S - \mathbf{b}\mathbf{b}^\top$ applied to $\mathbf{X}$ is not standard. More commonly, alignment measures project the subgradient onto the inlier subspace and then orthogonalize with respect to $\mathbf{b}$.

Given the reference to Tsakiris & Vidal (2018) and the convergence $\eta_{\mathbf{X}} \to 0$ under uniform data, it’s likely the definition is as intended, though non-standard. The context resolves the issue: it's a technical tool to bound perturbations. So while initially concerning, it appears consistent with the authors' framework.

comments.title: Inconsistent Use of $\mathbf{B}[0]$ vs $\widehat{\mathbf{B}}[0]$ in Theorem 5 and Lemma 4
comments.quote: Let _**B**_[ˆ][0] _∈_ R [D][×][c][′] ... A PSGM algorithm initialized with _**B**_[0]
comments.explanation:
In Lemma 4 and Theorem 5, the initial matrix is first defined as $\widehat{\mathbf{B}}[0]$, with hat notation consistent with other iterates $\widehat{\mathbf{b}}_i[k]$, which are normalized. However, in the statement of Theorem 5, the initialization is referred to as $\mathbf{B}[0]$ (without hat) in the second sentence. This is inconsistent.

Given that $\widehat{\mathbf{b}}_i[k]$ denotes the projected vector (after normalization), $\widehat{\mathbf{B}}[0]$ likely means the matrix of normalized initial vectors. But then referring to $\mathbf{B}[0]$ without hat suggests a different object—perhaps the unprojected version. Since all initial vectors are sampled from $\mathbb{S}^{D-1}$, $\mathbf{B}[0] = \widehat{\mathbf{B}}[0]$ in this case.

Hence, the inconsistency is not mathematical, but notational. It risks confusion in contexts where projection matters (e.g., discrete updates), but here it's harmless. Still, in a formal theorem, consistent notation is expected. This is likely a typo, but worth noting as underscoring the need for careful variable tracking.

comments.title: Inconsistent Initialization Description in Figure 2 Caption
comments.quote: Fig. 2(middle)) even without knowing the true value of _c_ , performing similarly to RSGM when the latter knows beforehand the correct codimension (Fig. 2(left)).
comments.explanation: At first glance, the caption of Figure 2 seems to mislabel the subfigures: it refers to 'Fig. 2(middle)' as the performance of DPCP-PSGM with overestimated c'=10, but earlier in the text it says 'left' corresponds to DPCP-PSGM with c'=10. This caused confusion about which panel is which. However, checking the actual figure description—'DPCP-PSGM... c'=10 (left)', 'RSGM with true c (middle)', and 'RSGM with c'=10 (right)'—reveals that the textual reference to '(middle)' when discussing DPCP-PSGM is clearly an error. The sentence meant to say that DPCP-PSGM (left) performs similarly to RSGM with correct c (middle), but instead mistakenly called the DPCP-PSGM result 'middle'. Given that both algorithms are being compared under proper conditions, this is purely a labeling inconsistency in the prose. Since it could mislead a reader into thinking the results are reversed, especially when visually comparing panels, and given that this affects the interpretation of a key empirical comparison, the issue remains problematic despite being resolvable by close inspection.

comments.title: Misleading Prose on Convergence of Discrete DPCP Iterates
comments.quote: Second, we analyze the discrete version which is more challenging, yet of more practical interest, showing that iterates of DPCP-PSGM converge to a scaled and perturbed version of the initial matrix **B**[0].
comments.explanation: The phrase 'scaled and perturbed version of the initial matrix **B**[0]' initially raised concern because the formal analysis later shows convergence not to a direct transformation of **B**[0], but rather to **P**_{S^\perp}(**B**_0) minus a perturbation matrix \widehat{\mathbf{\Delta}}—a projection followed by asymptotic error accumulation, not a simple scaling. After reviewing Lemma 4 and Theorem 5, it's clear that the limit depends only on the projection of **B**[0] onto S^\perp and the limiting perturbations \hat{\boldsymbol{\delta}}_i, not on B[0] itself in full. Thus, calling it a 'scaled and perturbed version of **B**[0]' is inaccurate and oversimplifies the geometric transformation involved. While 'scaled' might loosely refer to directional dependence via projection, and 'perturbed' to \widehat{\mathbf{\Delta}}, there is no uniform scaling of the full B[0], and the subspace projection fundamentally alters its structure. This risks misleading readers into thinking the output is a small deviation from the initialization, whereas in fact components in S are entirely suppressed. Given that the statement appears in the contributions list and shapes reader expectations, it constitutes a moderate overstatement.

comments.title: Inconsistent Use of Sgn in Outlier/Inlier Averages
comments.quote: \mathbf{x}_{\mathbf{b}_i} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{j=1}^N \mathrm{Sgn}(\mathbf{b}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j) \mathbf{x}_j
comments.explanation: The use of \mathrm{Sgn}(\cdot), presumably the sign function returning ±1, in the definition of \mathbf{x}_{\mathbf{b}_i} is standard in DPCP literature to approximate the subgradient of |b^⊤x|. However, in the expression, the output is then multiplied by \mathbf{x}_j — a vector — resulting in a vector with reversed direction based on the sign. While this matches prior work (e.g., Tsakiris & Vidal 2018), the OCR-rendered version uses \mathrm{Sgn}, whereas in many contexts such functions are denoted \text{sign} or \mathrm{sign}. But more critically, when comparing to the continuous limit (e.g., in Section 4.1), the expected value of \mathrm{Sgn}(\mathbf{b}^\top \mathbf{x}) \mathbf{x} under uniform distribution in a subspace is known to align with P_{S^\perp}(\mathbf{b}), which justifies the algorithm’s direction. However, the factor of 1/N is applied outside, implying an average — this is correct. No mathematical inconsistency was found across definitions, though the notation assumes readers recognize \mathrm{Sgn} as sign. Since this is conventional in the cited works and not contradicted by later analysis, and because the OCR output matches LaTeX rendering closely, this is not an error. Therefore, no flag is raised after resolution.

comments.title: Ambiguous Step Size Rule Reference in Theorem 1
comments.quote: then the iterates **b**[k] converge to a normal vector of S.
comments.explanation: The step size rule (8) mentioned in Theorem 1 is not included in the provided context, making it impossible to verify whether the stated convergence depends on unmentioned constraints (e.g., summability conditions, decay rate). However, the paper states: 'we assume that the step size has been selected such that existence of the limit is guaranteed' (footnote 2), indicating awareness of technical requirements. Moreover, in related work (Zhu et al., 2018), piecewise geometric decay with β < 1 is known to ensure convergence. Given that this is an informal version of Theorem 3 from prior work and that the full version is referred to in the appendix (which exists), the omission is intentional. Thus, while the current passage underspecifies a critical component of the algorithm, it is not underspecified in the full paper. No flag is warranted based on the given context.

comments.title: Confusing Description of Outlier Ratio in Recovery Experiment
comments.quote: two different outlier’s ratios _r_ , defined as _r_ = _MM_ + _N_[, namely] _[ r]_[=][0] _[.]_[6][ and] _[ r]_[=][0] _[.]_[7]
comments.explanation: The expression '_r_ = _MM_ + _N_' is an OCR artifact clearly meant to be 'r = M / (M + N)', as confirmed both by standard outlier ratio definitions in RSR and by the values r = 0.6 and 0.7, which only make sense as proportions. The duplication of 'M' is likely due to poor spacing in the source PDF ('M' followed by 'M+N' misread as 'MM + N'). This makes the ratio appear to be M*M + N, which is nonsensical. Context resolves this quickly: later text refers to 'outliers’ ratios equal to 0.7', and such values only make sense as fractions. Nevertheless, without that context, a careless reader might be confused—though not seriously misled, since 0.6 and 0.7 cannot result from M*M + N unless M and N are tiny. Given that this is a mechanical OCR failure and not a conceptual or authorial error, and that it appears in a clearly corrupted fragment, it does not reflect on the paper’s substance. Still, had it gone unnoticed, it could momentarily disrupt understanding.

comments.title: Rank Recovery Claim Lacks Explicit Connection to Rank Estimation Method
comments.quote: The results corroborate the theory showing that the DPCP-PSGM with random initialization biases the solutions of **B**[ˆ] towards matrices with rank _c_.
comments.explanation: While the claim that DPCP-PSGM recovers rank _c_ is central to the paper’s contribution, the passage does not specify *how* the estimated codimension is extracted from **B**[ˆ]. Is it via rank thresholding (e.g., counting singular values above a tolerance)? Or by checking when new iterates become linearly dependent? This is not described in the simulation section, nor in the caption of Fig. 3. Although from the theoretical results (Lemma 6, Theorem 7) one infers that the limiting **B*** should have rank _c_ due to the full-rank condition on **B**_0 − \widehat{\mathbf{\Delta}}, the empirical validation in Fig. 3 assumes the reader knows how rank was numerically estimated. In practice, determining numerical rank is non-trivial, especially near the threshold of recovery. Without mentioning the threshold or method (e.g., 'we take rank as number of singular values > 1e-6'), reproducibility is compromised. Given that this is a core claim—estimating unknown _c_—and the method for doing so is omitted, this qualifies as underspecification.

progressive__glm-4.6.label: Progressive (glm-4.6)
progressive__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: This paper presents a well-motivated and potentially significant contribution to the field of robust subspace recovery by addressing the critical limitation of prior DPCP methods, namely the need for *a priori* knowledge of the subspace

comments.title: Undefined parameter pc_D in equation (11)
comments.explanation: The parameter 'pc_D' appears in equation (11) without any definition in the provided context. This seems to be a crucial quantity for analyzing the convergence behavior, as it determines the scaling factor of the projected iterates. I checked the surrounding text and earlier sections, but couldn't find where this parameter is defined. Without knowing what pc_D represents, readers cannot properly evaluate the convergence claim or understand the magnitude of the scaling factor.

comments.title: Undefined measures in continuous formulation
comments.explanation: The continuous formulation in equation (9) uses measures µ_{S^{D-1}} and µ_{S^{D-1}∩S} without defining them. While readers might infer these are uniform measures on the sphere and its intersection with the subspace, this should be explicitly stated. The nature of these measures is important for understanding the continuous approximation and its relationship to the discrete problem.

comments.title: Insufficient conditions for 'almost surely' convergence
comments.quote: will almost surely asymptotically converge to a matrix B̂_* ∈ R^{D×c′}
comments.explanation: Lemma 3 claims 'almost surely asymptotically converge' but doesn't specify the conditions under which this holds. The 'almost surely' qualifier typically refers to probability 1 with respect to the random initialization, but the lemma doesn't explicitly state what properties the random initialization must have (e.g., continuous distribution, support over the entire sphere, etc.). This leaves readers uncertain about the precise probabilistic guarantee.

comments.title: Inconsistent and confusing notation in permeance statistics definitions
comments.quote: c_X,min = N^(-1) min_{b ∈ S^{d-1} ∩ S} ||X^T b||_1 and c_X,max = N^(-1) max_{b ∈ S^{D-1} ∩ S} ||X^T b||_1
comments.explanation: The notation in these definitions is inconsistent and mathematically problematic. For c_X,min, the minimization is over b ∈ S^{d-1} ∩ S, while for c_X,max it's over b ∈ S^{D-1} ∩ S. The intersection notation '∩ S' doesn't make sense here - if S is a d-dimensional subspace, then S^{d-1} would typically denote the unit sphere in R^d, not in S. The authors likely meant: for c_X,min, minimize over the unit sphere within the subspace S (i.e., {b ∈ S : ||b||_2 = 1}), and for c_X,max, maximize over the unit sphere in the ambient space R^D. This appears to be an OCR error that creates confusion about the domain of optimization.

comments.title: Undefined projection operator
comments.explanation: The projection operator P_{S⊥} is used in equation (11) without explicit definition. While readers familiar with linear algebra would understand this as projection onto the orthogonal complement of S, the operator should be formally defined when first introduced, especially since it's crucial for the convergence analysis.

comments.title: Confusing statement about linear independence when c' ≤ c
comments.quote: These are all linearly independent when c' ≤ c and thus can span S⊥ when c' = c.
comments.explanation: The statement is logically inconsistent. If c' ≤ c (meaning we have fewer vectors than the dimension of S⊥), then the vectors cannot span S⊥ unless c' = c. The authors likely meant to distinguish between three cases: 1) c' < c: vectors are linearly independent but don't span S⊥, 2) c' = c: vectors are linearly independent and span S⊥, 3) c' > c: we get redundant vectors. The current phrasing could mislead readers about the properties of the method when underestimating the codimension.

comments.title: Grammatical issue in description of continuous version analysis
comments.quote: Specifically, we prove that the DPCP problem in (3) can be solved via a projected subgradient algorithm that implicitly biases solutions towards low-rank B matrices whose columns are the projections of the randomly initialized columns of B0 onto S⊥.
comments.explanation: The sentence structure makes it appear that the columns of 'low-rank B matrices' are projections of B0, which is grammatically incorrect and confusing. The authors likely meant that the solution matrix B̂ has columns that are projections of the initialized columns. This could be clarified by restructuring the sentence to clearly separate the description of the algorithm's bias from the description of the solution's properties.

comments.title: Vague description of convergence behavior
comments.quote: showing that iterates of DPCP-PSGM converge to a scaled and perturbed version of the initial matrix B0.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'scaled and perturbed version' is imprecise for a technical paper. What constitutes the 'perturbation'? Is this a formal result with bounded error terms or an empirical observation? While this appears in an overview section where some imprecision is acceptable, the language should be more specific about the nature of this convergence behavior, or at least reference where precise details will be provided.

comments.title: Formula error in Lemma 4
comments.explanation: The formula in Lemma 4 contains a mathematical error. It states that the converged vector b̂*[i] equals the norm of some projection: b̂*[i] = ∥P_S⊥(b̂[0]_i)∥_2. This is mathematically incorrect because it equates a vector to a scalar (a norm). Based on the context of the paper and equation (11), the authors likely intended to express that the converged vector is the normalized projection of the initial vector onto S⊥, which should be written as: b̂*[i] = P_S⊥(b̂[0]_i)/∥P_S⊥(b̂[0]_i)∥_2. This appears to be an OCR error where the normalization was incorrectly placed.

comments.title: Redundant projection in Lemma 4 convergence formula
comments.explanation: The formula in Lemma 4 contains what appears to be redundant projections: P_{S⊥}(P_{S⊥}(b_i^{[0]})). Since P_{S⊥} is a projection operator, applying it twice to the same vector yields the same result as applying it once (P_{S⊥}^2 = P_{S⊥}). This might be a typo, or it could indicate something more subtle about the algorithm's behavior. If intentional, it should be clarified why the double projection is shown.

comments.title: Missing PSGM algorithm steps
comments.quote: performs the following steps per iteration[1]
comments.explanation: The text references 'steps given in (10)' for the PSGM algorithm, but equation (10) is not shown in this passage. While the general description mentions 'a subgradient step followed by a projection onto the sphere', the specific formulation is crucial for understanding the theoretical analysis that follows, especially the derivation in equation (11). This makes it difficult to fully verify the technical claims about convergence.

comments.title: Underspecified probabilistic condition in Theorem 7
comments.explanation: Theorem 7 presents a probabilistic condition but doesn't specify how to compute this probability or what values of δ are appropriate. The condition P[σ_c′(B[0]) > ∥Δ̂∥_2] ≥ 1 − δ is central to the theorem's guarantee, but without guidance on how to evaluate this probability or choose δ, readers cannot practically apply this result. The authors should provide either: 1) a bound on this probability in terms of known quantities, 2) guidance on selecting δ, or 3) a reference to where this computation is explained. While this might be addressed elsewhere in the paper, its absence here makes the theorem incomplete as stated.

comments.title: Potentially confusing notation in equation (11)
comments.explanation: The second part of equation (11) states that P_S⊥(b̂[k+1]_i) = P_S⊥(P_S_{D-1}(b̂[k+1]_i)). This equality holds only because b̂[k+1]_i is already normalized (as per Algorithm 1), but this isn't immediately obvious to readers. In general, P_S⊥(P_S_{D-1}(v)) = P_S⊥(v)/∥v∥ ≠ P_S⊥(v). The authors should add a brief clarification that this equality holds due to the normalization step in Algorithm 1, or rewrite it as P_S⊥(b̂[k+1]_i) = P_S⊥(b̂[k+1]_i)/∥b̂[k+1]_i∥_2 with a note that ∥b̂[k+1]_i∥_2 = 1. While technically correct, the current notation could mislead readers about the general relationship between these projection operators.

comments.title: Ambiguous claim about 100% success rate
comments.quote: It can be observed that DPCP-PSGM achieves 100% for all different codimensions for r = 0.6.
comments.explanation: The text claims DPCP-PSGM 'achieves 100%' but doesn't explicitly state what metric reaches 100%. While it's implied to be the success rate in correctly estimating the codimension across trials, this should be explicitly stated for clarity. A reader might wonder if it refers to accuracy, precision, recall, or some other metric.

comments.title: Typo in Figure 3 caption
comments.quote: Estimated by DPCP-PSGM codimensions for two difdimensions
comments.explanation: The word 'difdimensions' appears to be a typographical error, likely intended to be 'different dimensions'. This is a minor OCR or editing error that doesn't affect the meaning but should be corrected.

comments.title: Underspecified codimension estimation method
comments.quote: we report the estimated codimensions obtained by DPCP-PSGM for 10 independent trials of the experiments.
comments.explanation: While the theory establishes that rank(B̂) = c, the practical method for estimating the codimension from the computed B̂ matrix is not specified. In numerical implementations, rank estimation requires thresholding singular values or applying another criterion to handle finite-precision computations. Without specifying this methodological detail, readers cannot fully reproduce the experimental results.

This leaves readers uncertain about the precise probabilistic guarantee.

comments.title: Confusing notation in permeance statistics definition
comments.explanation: The notation S^{d-1}∩S in the definition of c_{X,min} is confusing. I believe the authors mean the unit sphere restricted to the subspace S, but this notation could be clearer. A better notation might be S^{d-1}_S or {b∈S : ||b||_2=1}.

While readers familiar with linear algebra would understand this as projection onto the orthogonal complement of S, the operator should be formally defined when first introduced, especially since it's crucial for the convergence analysis.

comments.title: Confusing statement about linear independence when c' ≤ c
comments.quote: These are all linearly independent when c' ≤ c and thus can span S⊥ when c' = c.
comments.explanation: The statement is logically inconsistent. If c' ≤ c (meaning we have fewer vectors than the dimension of S⊥), then the vectors cannot span S⊥ unless c' = c. The authors likely meant to distinguish between three cases: 1) c' < c: vectors are linearly independent but don't span S⊥, 2) c' = c: vectors are linearly independent and span S⊥, 3) c' > c: we get redundant vectors. The current phrasing could mislead readers about the properties of the method when underestimating the codimension.

comments.title: Grammatical issue in description of continuous version analysis
comments.quote: Specifically, we prove that the DPCP problem in (3) can be solved via a projected subgradient algorithm that implicitly biases solutions towards low-rank B matrices whose columns are the projections of the randomly initialized columns of B0 onto S⊥.
comments.explanation: The sentence structure makes it appear that the columns of 'low-rank B matrices' are projections of B0, which is grammatically incorrect and confusing. The authors likely meant that the solution matrix B̂ has columns that are projections of the initialized columns. This could be clarified by restructuring the sentence to clearly separate the description of the algorithm's bias from the description of the solution's properties.

comments.title: Vague description of convergence behavior

comments.explanation: The phrase 'scaled and perturbed version' is imprecise for a technical paper. What constitutes the 'perturbation'? Is this a formal result with bounded error terms or an empirical observation? While this appears in an overview section where some imprecision is acceptable, the language should be more specific about the nature of this convergence behavior, or at least reference where precise details will be provided.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation in permeance statistics definitions
comments.quote: c_X,min = N^(-1) min_{b ∈ S^{d-1} ∩ S} ||X^T b||_1 and c_X,max = N^(-1) max_{b ∈ S^{D-1} ∩ S} ||X^T b||_1
comments.explanation: The notation in these definitions is inconsistent and mathematically problematic. For c_X,min, the minimization is over b ∈ S^{d-1} ∩ S, while for c_X,max it's over b ∈ S^{D-1} ∩ S. The intersection notation '∩ S' doesn't make sense here - if S is a d-dimensional subspace, then S^{d-1} would typically denote the unit sphere in R^d, not in S. The authors likely meant: for c_X,min, minimize over the unit sphere within the subspace S (i.e., {b ∈ S : ||b||_2 = 1}), and for c_X,max, maximize over the unit sphere in the ambient space R^D. This appears to be an OCR error that creates confusion about the domain of optimization.

This is particularly important since equation (11) is a key step in the theoretical analysis showing how PSGM iterates behave when projected onto the subspace.

This appears to be an OCR error where the normalization was incorrectly placed.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The introduction provides an exceptionally clear and compelling narrative for the proposed work, effectively bridging the gap between existing Dual Principal Component Pursuit (DPCP) methods and the practical limitation of needing prior knowledge of subspace dimension. The writing is technically precise, well-structured, and does an excellent job of positioning the research within the context of established literature, particularly in how it highlights the specific constraint of previous methods (requiring $c$ to be known) and offers a conceptually elegant solution through the parallelization of PSGM. The most significant strength is the clarity with which the authors transition from the limitations of the current state-of-the-art to their novel contribution; if the subsequent sections match this standard of exposition and provide sufficiently rigorous proofs for the emergence of "implicit rank regularization" from random initialization, the paper is well-positioned for strong reception. No critical flaws are apparent in this opening, though the potential for redundancy in the parallel instances—and the theoretical guarantee that they will yield a basis rather than redundant vectors—will be the key technical threshold the authors must clear to justify the contribution.

comments.title: Mathematical Notation Inconsistency for Data Matrix and Dimensions
comments.quote: Specifically, we propose a very simple algorithm based on running multiple instances of a projected sub-gradient descent method (PSGM)... the true dimension of the subspace is known.
comments.explanation: The manuscript inconsistently defines the data matrix $X$ (switching between $X$, $\tilde{X}$, and implied full-set matrices) and uses 'dimension' versus 'codimension' ($c$ vs $c'$) interchangeably. This lack of formal definition and inconsistent terminology creates confusion for readers attempting to implement or verify the DPCP objective function.

comments.title: OCR-Induced Garbled and Incomprehensible Technical Statements
comments.quote: Specifically, we prove that the DPCP problem in (3) can be solved via a B ˆprojected ∈ R [D][×][c][′] subgradientwhose columns are the projections of the randomly initialized columns ofalgorithm...
comments.explanation: Significant OCR errors in sections 1 and 14 have rendered key technical assertions about the algorithm's bias and structure unintelligible. The fragmented and garbled text obscures the core contribution regarding the subgradient method and requires re-editing for technical accuracy.

comments.title: Ambiguous Mathematical Definitions in Lemma 4
comments.explanation: The expression provided for the recovered vector in Lemma 4 is mathematically incoherent due to redundant notation and undefined operators (e.g., 'PPS⊥S⊥'). This makes it impossible to verify the stated result, as the expression functions more like an OCR artifact than a formal definition.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Continuous Projection Step
comments.explanation: The definition of the projection operator $P_{S}D^{-1}$ in Equation (11) is non-standard and undefined. The mathematical logic appears circular and lacks the rigor necessary to justify the paper’s claim that these expressions describe the scaled recovery process.

comments.title: Logical Gap in Theorem 7 Concerning Rank Sufficiency
comments.quote: a sufficient condition for this to hold is that the matrix A = B[0] − Δˆ is full-rank.
comments.explanation: The transition from Lemma 6 (conditions on $A$) to Theorem 7 contains a logical gap. The proof fails to justify why the perturbation $\Deltaˆ$ does not collapse the rank of the randomly initialized matrix $B_0$. Crucially, the statement ends abruptly and fails to provide the full conclusion of the theorem, leaving the justification for the main convergence claim incomplete.

comments.title: Ambiguous Figure Captions and Labeling
comments.quote: Figure 2: Distances of the recovered Bˆ from the true orthogonal complements...
comments.explanation: The text referencing Figure 2 and Figure 3 contains internal contradictions where the descriptive labels (e.g., 'left', 'middle') do not match the panel definitions. Additionally, the caption for Figure 3 contains a nonsensical equation ($r = M = 0.6 / (M + N)$) instead of the defined ratio $r=M/(M+N)$. These errors hinder the reader's ability to interpret the experimental results accurately.

comments.title: Mathematical Notation Inconsistency for Data Matrix X
comments.quote: Specifically, we propose a very simple algorithm based on running multiple instances of a projected sub-gradient descent method (PSGM), with each problem instance seeking to find one vector in the null space of the subspace. ... 3 DUAL PRINCIPAL COMPONENT PURSUIT AND THE PROJECTED SUBGRADIENT METHOD ... We re-write the DPCP formulation given in (1) as
comments.explanation: In the introduction, the authors define a data matrix $\tilde{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{D \times (N+M)}$ which is formed by concatenating inliers $X$ and outliers $O$. However, Equation (1) (which is implied to be referenced when the text mentions re-writing) and the subsequent subgradient step (Eq. 5) reference $X^\top b$. It is standard in DPCP literature to use $X$ to denote the data matrix. The transition between $\tilde{X}$ (the oracle containing outliers) and $X$ (the inlier matrix) is occasionally blurred in the definitions. While the context implies $X$ in the equation refers to the full set of points (inliers + outliers), the manuscript lacks a formal definition of the modified data matrix used in the objective function of (5), potentially confusing a reader trying to implement the loss function.

comments.title: Scope of the 'High Relative Dimension' Claim
comments.quote: However, these theoretical guarantees often require the dimension of the subspace, $d$, to be significantly less than the ambient dimension of the data, $D$, and these methods are not suitable for the more challenging regime of subspaces of high relative dimension (i.e., when $D[d][≈]_[1][).]$
comments.explanation: The OCR-damaged text $D[d][≈]_[1]$ is clearly meant to denote $d \approx D$. While the intent is clear from context, the claim that existing convex formulations (e.g., LRR or RPCA) are 'not suitable' for the high relative dimension regime is a strong claim that contradicts some literature (e.g., Candès et al., 2011, which discusses the recovery of high-dimensional subspaces under certain incoherence and distribution assumptions). The authors should clarify if they mean 'computationally unsuitable' or 'theoretically impossible' to remain precise in the Introduction.

comments.title: OCR-induced garbled text in Section 1
comments.quote: Specifically, we prove that the DPCP problem in (3) can be solved via a _**B**_ ˆprojected _∈_ R _[D][×][c][′]_ subgradientwhose columns are the projections of the randomly initialized columns ofalgorithm that implicitly biases solutions towards low-rank _**B**_ matrices[0] onto _S⊥_ .
comments.explanation: This sentence is severely corrupted by OCR errors, causing a complete loss of grammatical and technical coherence. The phrase 'subgradientwhose columns are the projections of the randomly initialized columns ofalgorithm' is unintelligible. While the context provides the intended meaning—that the subgradient algorithm's dynamics produce an implicit bias—the lack of a readable, precise statement of the result in this summary point weakens the presentation of the main contribution.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Linear Independence' claim
comments.quote: These are all _linearly independent_ when _c[′] ≤ c_ and thus can span _S⊥_ when _c[′]_ = _c_ .
comments.explanation: The text implies that if one uses random initializations and runs PSGM, the results are 'linearly independent' if the number of instances (codimension bound) does not exceed the true codimension. However, this is a strong probabilistic claim that depends on the geometry of the initialization relative to the subspace. The text presents this as a near-certainty ('will return') without mentioning the necessary conditions (like the span of initial vectors) that guarantee linear independence in the resulting null space basis.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for ambient/subspace dimension
comments.quote: the true dimension of the subspace is known. In this paper, we show that if the orthogonality constraints –adopted in previous DPCP formulations– are relaxed ... DPCP can provably recover a subspace of _unknown dimension_ .
comments.explanation: The authors define the problem using 'codimension' (c = D - d) throughout, yet the Abstract and Introduction use 'dimension' and 'codimension' interchangeably. Specifically, the paper claims to recover a subspace of 'unknown dimension' while the technical focus is on 'unknown codimension'. While this is common in some literature, it creates a lack of precision, especially when the paper explicitly defines and uses 'codimension' for the algorithm parameter c'.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in the definition of rank and span
comments.quote: Specifically, we prove that the DPCP problem in (3) can be solved via a **B** ˆprojected ∈ R [D][×][c][′] subgradientwhose columns are the projections of the randomly initialized columns ofalgorithm that implicitly biases solutions towards low-rank **B** matrices[0] onto S⊥.
comments.explanation: This sentence (found in the Contributions section) contains significant OCR-related garbling that makes the description of the algorithm and the claim about rank incomprehensible. While the surrounding text clarifies the intent, the fragmented phrasing 'a **B** ˆprojected ∈ R [D][×][c][′] subgradientwhose columns are the projections of the randomly initialized columns ofalgorithm' creates a high risk of reader confusion regarding the mathematical procedure being proposed. It mixes the definition of the matrix with the process description in an unreadable way.

comments.title: Unexplained notation and missing equation reference in Lemma 4
comments.explanation: The formula provided for the recovered normal vector in Lemma 4 contains redundant notation (e.g., repeating terms like **b** [ˆ][0] i ) and uses 'PPS⊥S⊥' which appears to be a typo for a projection operator or a composition of projections, but it is not formally defined. Given that this lemma links the initialization to the final result, the notation is too opaque to verify the correctness of the stated result.

comments.title: Ambiguous projection step in the continuous version
comments.explanation: The notation used in equation (11) is internally inconsistent. The first part defines a transformation of the projection, while the second part uses P S D− 1 in a way that is not defined in the paper (P with subscript S D-1 is not a standard projector compared to P sub-S_perp used elsewhere). Furthermore, the expression appears recursive or circular, making it unclear whether this is a derivation step or a definition. Without a clear definition of the operator P S D− 1, the logic supporting the 'scaled versions' claim is difficult to follow.

comments.title: Notation ambiguity in Lemma 4 projection expression
comments.explanation: The expression inside the norm operator contains `_**b**_[ˆ] _**b**_[ˆ][0] _i_[0] _i_[`. This notation seems to be an OCR artifact or a typo for the projection of the initial vector `_P_ S^⊥ ( _**b**_[0] _i _ )`. Given the context of Section 4.1, the intended expression likely relates the learned vector to the projection of the initial vector into the orthogonal complement. The current form is mathematically garbled and uninterpretable as written.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of average inliers/outliers terms
comments.quote: where _**x b** i_ and _o_ _**b** i_ are called as _average inliers_ and _average outliers_ terms, defined as _**x b** i_ = _N_ 1  _Nj_ =1 _[Sgn]_[(] _**[b]** i[⊤]_ _**[x]**[ j]_[)] _**[x]**[ j]_[and] _**[ o][b]** i_[=] _M_ 1  _Mj_ =1 _[Sgn]_[(] _**[b]** i[⊤]_ _**[o]**[j]_[)] _**[o]**[j]_[.]

Based on the context of the DPCP objective (sum over N+M points) and standard notation for empirical means (1/N), it is highly likely these were intended to be '1/N' and '1/M'. The missing fraction bar/slash makes the expression dimensionally inconsistent in a formal setting.

comments.title: Logical gap regarding rank sufficiency in Theorem 7
comments.quote: a sufficient condition for this to hold is that the matrix _**A**_ = _**B**_[0] _−_ **∆**[ˆ] is full-rank.
comments.explanation: The authors link the span of the learned basis to the full-rank property of A = B_0 - Delta_hat. While Lemma 6 addresses the condition under which A is full-rank, Theorem 7 ends abruptly and does not explicitly provide the inequality (18) or the full statement of the theorem's conclusion, despite referencing it. The logic assumes that if B_0 is sufficiently 'well-behaved' (random), the perturbation Delta_hat will not reduce the rank. The proof for why the perturbation Delta_hat (which depends on the discrete approximation of the gradient) does not collapse the rank of a randomly drawn B_0 is non-trivial; the jump from Lemma 6 to Theorem 7 appears to skip the justification of why the bound on Delta_hat holds.

Based on the context of the previous lines (Equation 11) and the purpose of the limit, 'PPS⊥S⊥' is likely meant as P_{S^⊥}(b_i^{(0)}), where P_{S^⊥} is the projection onto the orthogonal complement of the subspace S. The expression '(( b ˆ b ˆ[0] i [0] i ) )' appears to be a garbled version of P_{S^⊥}(b_i^{(0)}). While I can infer the intent that the limit is a normalized version of the initial vector projected onto S^⊥, the current notation is mathematically incoherent.

However, in 'Section 2: Robust Subspace Recovery (RSR)' of the paper, the authors discuss the complexity of RANSAC and state: '...when the number of outliers is high...' without formalizing the relationship to the total sample size. comments.title: Ambiguity in 'c' vs 'c_D' notation
comments.explanation: In the summary, the update equation uses 'c_D'. Later, the text mentions '$c_D$' as an average height of the unit hemisphere.
